name: Angliru and Alto del Cordal Loop from Mieres
name_original: El Angliru – circular desde Mieres de Camín por el Alto del Cordal
description: Tackle the legendary El Angliru – circular desde Mieres de Camín por el Alto del Cordal, a difficult 41.6-mile road cycling route.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Mieres are characterized by mountainous terrain and deep valleys, offering varied challenges for cyclists. The region is situated in a valley bisected by the Caudal River, providing a scenic backdrop for rides. Mieres is part of the Cuencas Mineras Protected Landscape, featuring a blend of natural beauty and cultural heritage. The area includes significant climbs and passes, making it a destination for road cyclists seeking elevation gain. Best road cycling routes around Mieres The most popular road cycling route is Angliru and Alto del Cordal Loop from Mieres, a 41.6 miles (66.9 km) difficult trail that takes 4 hours 28 minutes to complete, featuring significant elevation gains over iconic climbs. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Cubilla Pass – Campomanes loop from Santa Cruz, a difficult 53.5 miles (86.1 km) path that traverses mountain passes with challenging ascents. Local road cyclists also love the Alto de Gamoniteiro – Alto de La Cobertoria loop from Estación d'Uxo, a 30.7 miles (49.5 km) trail leading through high mountain terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 19 minutes. Road cycling around Mieres is defined by mountainous terrain, river valleys, and diverse forests, including alders and ash. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Mieres are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 16 reviews. More than 1,800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Mieres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Mieres?
A: Mieres offers a wide variety of road cycling routes, with over 30 options available on komoot. These routes cater to different skill levels, including both moderate and difficult challenges.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of road cycling in Mieres?
A: Road cycling in Mieres is characterized by its mountainous terrain, offering significant elevation gains. The majority of routes are classified as either moderate or difficult, with 15 difficult routes and 16 moderate routes listed on komoot, making it an ideal destination for cyclists seeking a challenge.

Q: Are there any iconic climbs for experienced road cyclists in the Mieres area?
A: Absolutely. Mieres is home to the legendary &lt;strong&gt;Alto de l'Angliru&lt;/strong&gt;, a challenging climb often featured in the Vuelta a España. This ascent spans 12 kilometers with a staggering 1258 vertical meters, featuring average gradients of 13% in its second half and ramps exceeding 20%. You can experience a significant portion of this climb on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1077471311/angliru-and-alto-del-cordal-loop-from-mieres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Angliru and Alto del Cordal Loop from Mieres&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of landscapes and natural features can I expect to see while cycling around Mieres?
A: The region offers diverse and picturesque landscapes. You'll cycle through river valleys bisected by the Caudal River, past diverse forests with species like alders, ash, and birch, and within the &lt;strong&gt;Cuencas Mineras Protected Landscape&lt;/strong&gt;. Routes often provide stunning vistas of the Urbiés mountain range and dramatic peaks within the nearby &lt;strong&gt;Las Ubiñas Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt;.

Q: Are there any specific mountain passes or climbs I should look out for?
A: Mieres is rich in mountain passes. Besides Alto de l'Angliru, you can explore climbs like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5619300" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Santo Emiliano&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3592919" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Cordal&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3592968" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de La Colladiella&lt;/a&gt;. Routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6971428" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Cubilla Pass – Campomanes loop from Santa Cruz&lt;/a&gt; traverse challenging mountain passes.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Mieres?
A: The road cycling routes around Mieres are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 16 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the challenging climbs, the stunning mountain scenery, and the well-maintained roads that make for an exhilarating riding experience.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available from Mieres?
A: Yes, many routes around Mieres are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40358533" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mieres Town Center – Pola de Lena loop from Mieres del Camín&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a moderate challenge, and the more demanding &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40370037" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Angliru Summit – Angliru Summit Monument loop from Mieres-Vasco&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Mieres?
A: While specific weather conditions can vary, the mountainous terrain suggests that spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer and the colder, potentially snowy conditions of winter at higher elevations. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out.

Q: Are there any attractions or points of interest near the road cycling routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can find cultural and historical points of interest. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4395523" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of San Salvador in Oviedo&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4494047" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Isidoro el Real Church&lt;/a&gt; are within reach. For those interested in unique natural formations, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/297709" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Xanas&lt;/a&gt; offers a scenic gorge nearby.

Q: Are there any less strenuous or moderate road cycling options around Mieres?
A: While Mieres is known for its challenging climbs, there are also moderate options. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40358533" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mieres Town Center – Pola de Lena loop from Mieres del Camín&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that provides a good balance of distance and elevation without the extreme gradients of the major climbs. The region offers 16 moderate routes in total.

Q: Can I find routes that combine road cycling with other types of biking, like gravel or mountain biking?
A: While this guide focuses on road cycling, the region does offer diverse terrain. For example, the &lt;strong&gt;Vía Verde del Valle de Turón&lt;/strong&gt; is a 12.96 km route that is ideal for gravel or mountain biking, showcasing a blend of nature and the area's mining heritage. This provides an alternative for those looking to explore different surfaces.

Q: What is the average elevation gain on the more difficult routes?
A: The difficult routes around Mieres feature significant elevation gains. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1077471311/angliru-and-alto-del-cordal-loop-from-mieres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Angliru and Alto del Cordal Loop from Mieres&lt;/a&gt; has an elevation gain of over 2,100 meters, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31984117" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Gamoniteiro – Alto de La Cobertoria loop from Estación d'Uxo&lt;/a&gt; involves over 1,500 meters of climbing, making them truly challenging rides.
tips: 12 kilometers of track and 1258 vertical meters make the climb to Angliru a legendary challenge in road cycling. In the first half you can bounce around at an incline of around 8 percent, while in the second half you expect an average of 13 percent - with two ramps over 20 percent. Here it is no shame, if you have to get off the saddle.

The council of Riosa could be considered one of the epicenters of cycling in Asturias. At the foot of Angliru, one of the toughest ascents in the world, it is a crossing point for other mountain passes that have been part of the Vuelta a España route. From this point you can sense the hardness of the mythical Angliru with its impossible asphalt ramps between the mountains.
Starting point of several routes. good parking

faqs.question: How difficult is the El Angliru circular route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a difficult to very difficult road ride, demanding a very good fitness level. The ascent to El Angliru features extreme gradients, with the last 6 km averaging 13% and some ramps exceeding 20%, reaching a maximum of 23.5%. It's considered an 'HC' (Hors Catégorie) climb, the hardest category in professional cycling.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily follows well-paved, winding, and narrow roads. While the surfaces are generally in perfect condition, the challenge comes from the brutal gradients, particularly on the ascent to El Angliru and the demanding Alto del Cordal.

faqs.question: What can I expect to see along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience stunning Asturian scenery, characterized by lush greenery, alternating meadows, and wooded areas. As you climb, especially towards the Ascent to Alto de l'Angliru, you'll be rewarded with spectacular mountain views of the region. The upper sections of Angliru can often be dramatic, with cyclists riding into the clouds.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. The route is famous for the legendary Ascent to Alto de l'Angliru, often called the 'Olympus of cycling.' You'll also traverse the demanding Alto del Cordal, a historic pass. Near the summit of Angliru, there's a specific View of El Angliru offering panoramic vistas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the challenging nature and potential for dramatic weather, it's generally best to cycle this route during the warmer, drier months, typically late spring to early autumn. This avoids the worst of winter conditions and reduces the risk of challenging descents on wet roads.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts from Mieres de Camín. You should look for suitable public parking options within Mieres de Camín itself to begin your ride. Specific parking areas are usually available in the town center or near local amenities.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this public road route. It is freely accessible for cyclists.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for cycling with dogs?
faqs.answer: This route is an extremely challenging road cycling route designed for race bikes. Due to the high gradients, narrow roads, and significant traffic on certain sections, it is not suitable for cycling with dogs, even if they are in a trailer.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of the [CIMA AS17] Angliru * La Vega (Riosa), Camino del Salvador, and [CIMA AS17] Angliru * Santa Eulalia, among others.

faqs.question: What should I be aware of during the descent?
faqs.answer: While the climb is legendary, the descent from El Angliru can be equally challenging and requires extreme caution. Some parts have been improved with new tarmac, but it can still be fear-inducing, especially in wet conditions. Maintain focus and control, and be prepared for steep, winding sections.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is definitely not suitable for beginner cyclists. It is consistently classified as difficult to very difficult, with extreme gradients that require very good fitness, advanced cycling skills, and significant mental fortitude. Even professional cyclists have struggled on these climbs.

faqs.question: Are there any places to get provisions or refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through Mieres de Camín, where you can find cafes, pubs, and shops for provisions before or after your ride. However, once you begin the main climbs like Alto del Cordal and El Angliru, options become very limited or non-existent. It's crucial to carry sufficient water and nutrition for the entire duration of the ride.
